I assume it's obvious to everyone. All right, here's the first question. This is from Rich. How do you decide which value newsletter is to repurpose for your website? How do you think about that? Have you ever republished an entire series of V&Ls to your site? That seems like a great question for you to start answering, Andre. Let me answer the second part of that, which is easy. We do, and we have. Orphan, the one I can remember, because Mary asked a question in the comments and I responded and I reported it this morning, which is our durable business. We last year, we did a far part. It wasn't necessarily V&L. It was a series that spun off the V&L, which we then published to the website. So it's similar. We've never done more than one, I mean, more than two V&Ls as part of a lack of themed thing. Yeah. So I can share the link to the durable business one. I'll find that link in a sec when Sean's talking. As to how do you decide what to repurpose? The thing is, most of the stuff that we write is evergreen in nature. We don't tend to write stuff that's so technical that by the time we hit the publish button and send the email out, it's wasting away. It's degrading. We don't do that. So we can really publish every single newsletter that we send, and most emails that we send could easily end up on the website. But one of the things we do is we slightly modify them. So we'll take out any chatter that we've done that's specific to the emails, because there's a relationship that's already been established to everybody that's receiving our V&Ls. So we could have more of a lot harder thing. We could mention stuff that's happened. We can create some context that makes more sense to people that have been paying attention. But then when we repurpose those, we kind of strip out some of that stuff. So the article or the piece on the website is a standalone thing, minus all the stuff that could seem weird, that context stuff. And then one of the things that I'll mention, I mean that we add sometimes, when it makes sense, is if we've republished anything to the website, and it's thematically linked to something else that's maybe not obvious necessarily, we'll link that up in the photo. Like we will hard code. We're not using any plugins for this. We will say, if you enjoyed this or the insights over here, hit home with you. This is related, and it'll be a link to another article that's either literally relevant or completely thematically relevant. And we'll just link to that. So someone that's pulling on that thread and reading that thing, when they get to the bottom, there's more threads to pull, and they can go deeper down the revert form. Let me get that link. Wash, and charm us in. But my first reaction was, we don't really think about it this way. And then the moment I thought that, I realized, well, yeah, we have a bunch. We thought about it that way. So I think there are two ways you can think about this. When I say two, I'm going to get to 20 by the time I finish talking, probably. But the two broad ways to think about this are, assume from the beginning that you're going to want to publish on whatever content you're creating for your V&L on your site and act accordingly, or just create great content and then realize after the fact, oh, yeah, that really should go on the site. And that sounds absolutely idiotic. But it changes the dynamics a little bit. And I think the only reason I know that is this summer, we switched from a bi-weekly schedule to a weekly schedule. And we also, prior to that, we made this deliberate decision that we were going to be writing V&Ls that were really standalone articles and that we wrote them. The email was written knowing it would be published on the site as a piece of content. That's different than creating a value newsletter and then just dropping it in the site later when you're like, oh, this was a good series. It's worth publishing. It's just they have different dynamics. And of course, this is all dependent on having a type of newsletter that fits into this format where you're creating or you're curating content. So there are lots of decisions that go into this. But I think when you strip everything away, this is always true. When you strip everything away, the fundamental question that Rich has asked is how do you decide which V&L is to be purpose on your website? That question is actually further downstream from the more important question, which is how do you decide what you believe is valuable for your audience? That's the question to answer first. Everything else is mechanical at that point. It's just platform stuff. Like you decide something is that worth sharing with your audience, and then you just figure out what platforms are going, how you're going to share it. You're going to send it by email or you're going to post it on your website. Are you going to add it somewhere in a course? Is it going to be, are you going to put six or seven of them together and make them a free course? Is there part of that content that you realize could be tangential and you could run with a soap opera series like it opens the door or something? But it really, everything starts with this question of what is most valuable to my audience? And sometimes that's time sensitive. Certainly, if we're talking about email marketing in the fall of 2021, iOS 15 is just on everybody's mind. We can't, one of the two most important metrics for understanding email performance, one of those two open rates is going away. We can't use it anymore accurately. And the more time passes, the less accurate open rate becomes because email or Apple is essentially obscuring open rate by opening everybody, by sending the signal that everybody who's using Apple Mail has opened their email. So we can't trust that data anymore. So that's a timely, important piece of value to share with an audience now. And then oftentimes what we're sharing is timeless, right? Like the simple testing framework from Sesame Street. I wonder what if, let's try, right? That's not going to go away. That testing as a way to think about how to test ideas, that framework is not going to go away in the next three or four decades. So it's just, and it's valuable, right? That's everything comes back to this central idea of what is valuable, knowing what you know that your audience doesn't. When it's time to sit down and create something, whether it's a newsletter or a series, that doesn't matter. The first place to start is what would be interesting and valuable to my audience right now. But that's, I think to me that's the, that, that question is at the core of everything. When you answer that question, all the details reveal themselves. Anything else to add to that one? If I see you're just typing away here, what the hell are you all you're putting? I mean, I've mentioned a million times my second favorite quote is the E Cummins, always the beautiful answer who asks a more beautiful question. You know, it's, and I don't, I've heard, I know this is not my thinking, but at least 15 years ago, if not longer, I internalize this idea that the quality, I think this is like a Tony Robbins idea. I'm sure he's still from Jim Rohn or somebody else, but borrowed. Sorry. This idea that the quality of the questions you ask yourself determines the quality of your life. I think that's a Tony Robbins idea. And it's true, right? And I remember like some of the examples that were given. Again, I think it was Tony Robbins who, like, if you wake up every morning and you ask yourself, why does my life suck? Well, your brain is agnostic. It's just going to go look out and find reasons and tell you, well, your life sucks because, you know, but if you wake up every morning and you ask yourself, what, what could I do today that, you know, what, what can I do today that would be great? What can I be grateful for today? Like there's, it's just by changing the questions we ask, everything downstream changes as well. So I just, I love, to me, the most powerful thing I've ever stumbled on is, is the power of asking a really good question or looking for a really good question. When you find a really good question, everything after that just fundamentally changes. So having said that, that wasn't intended to be a setup, but I'll make it a setup. So the question, what to give away for free and emails is a shitty question. Right. That's just not a quality question because there's, it has too many implications, right? That there's somehow there's a difference between free value and paid for value. Or like that's just, it's just the wrong way to think about it. It's to reformulate this question, you know, ask yourself, what could I communicate to my audience today that could make their lives meaningfully better? Well, hell, that now you had a thousand answers that you could come up with and your brain just starts firing off those answers. And then you look for that. But when you try to bifurcate, like, oh, I want to, I want to give stuff away, but then I also have to make money. It creates a false dichotomy. Right. I mean, when you, there's a psychological effect. When, when you're giving things away and freely giving things away and really sharing insights that I don't think it makes somebody think like, wow, why bother buying anything from this person? It actually makes them think, well, if, if this is the free stuff, the paid stuff must be incredible. Right. I mean, it's, so there's, there's, I don't think there's a downside other than overwhelm when you give, you know, when you're, when you're really emphasizing the free content. I mean, yes, there are some frameworks I've heard in the past, which I don't like, you know, one that was popular for a long time is, you know, that you give away the why and you sell the how. But again, that's a bifurcation. I hate, I just gross, but oftentimes your audience needs to understand why and what, and often what, and often what your insight is, is that you're framing things for your audience in a way that they can understand it. I mean, that, that's me, and I realized, you know, I've been looking through the comments and hearing this a lot, that people are really struggling with what rises to the level of an insight versus just information and an insight. I don't think it's, I don't think it's black and white. An insight's different for different people. You know, some people have an aha moment with something that you feel like was just information. And then others look at something that you believe was an insight and they're like, oh yeah, of course, that makes sense. So it's, it's not as cut and dry. I mean, I think if we, if we want to talk in terms of frameworks, insights are more likely to be found in second and third order beyond thinking. Right. That's, that's where to go look for them. Right. And the example I think we've given here a few times, but, um, this observation that most people who are not going to buy, or most people who buy over the course of two years are not going to buy in 90 days. That's information. For some people, that's an insight. Um, but then when we get into second order thinking about that, um, in third order, fourth order thinking, that's where all the insights are. Like it's like, well, if that's true, then the real risk in the short term is that we screw up by trying to sell more to the few people who are, who are going to buy anyway, but then we're likely to undermine the potential for future sales that will happen if we just don't screw it up. But that's, that's an insight. That's a second order consequence of upstream information. So do you, do you give away insights? I'm sure it's the way I guess the best framework I can think about is if you're custom building the ideal prospect, you have all, you know, you have lots of time and you, and you could sit down and think, okay, what if for someone who is going to not only be ideal, a hell yes, a 10, no one, they, they see this offer that I have, what, what needs to be true before that happens? What needs to be true for them? Like what, what needs do they need to have? What do they need to feel like that they're capable of? Like what emotions line up with this decision and what, in order to get the most value from the offer that you have, what do they need to know and be prepared to do? Like make that list, really get clear on it and then give all of that away. Right. Because you're, you're making good customers. You're making good prospects. And why not? I mean, why wouldn't you? It's, it, and again, it gets, so this is where we get into the better questions. Instead of asking yourself, what, what do I give away free in my emails? Ask yourself, if I have somewhere between, you know, seven days and seven, seven, somewhere between seven and 700 days, that's approximately, that's within the framework of, you know, when someone's going to buy quickly or someone's going to buy over two years. So when I have seven to 700 days to make a perfect customer, what would I share and then just get after it, start sharing it. And some people are going to, in, you know, day seven, they're going to, oh, shit, this, I'm ready. I wanted this before I even got here. I'm ready to go. I believe all this, if I trust you, off we go, I'm buying today. And then someone else, you're going to build them incrementally, one email at a time, one good interaction at a time. And then day 700 arrives and all that work that you've put in has created this perfect customer and then they're ready at that point in time. So that's, that's the best way I would think about it. Just don't, don't think about it as like, oh, I'm giving away too much. Just give and educate and watch what happens because that it's magical when that happens. All right. And it's that can repel people. Even people who want to, who it could be an ideal customer, it can repel those people. So take your time. I'm really, I think trying to feed a single, like really to focus on, on crafting a single idea that somebody can understand at the end of, you know, whether it's 1500 words, whatever it is, but that, that you're leaving them with, with an idea in your, in your job is all of our jobs. When we're inside the tent of knowledge and communicate with people who are outside the tent of knowledge or, or making their way into the tent, our job is not to make it look complicated. Our job is to make it look simple. And when I say simple, I don't mean simplistic. It's, this is my, my favorite quote is all over window homes. I would not give a fig for the simplicity on this side of complexity, but I would give my right arm for the simplicity on the far side of complexity. So we're in the far side of complexity business. All of us are. We're, it's from the outside, things look complex. And when you spend a really long time inside a space or you've done the work to understand a space, the byproduct of that should be able to draw attention to some of the simpler truths in there to take lots of things that seem like they're different and collapse them into either a single theme or a single idea. And perhaps even bring that all the way down to the discipline itself. And this is something that Andre and I have talked about inspired by a podcast I listened to several months ago that we think we can reduce marketing down to two words and like that, like that's the ultimate. And then, then you sort of build out from there. And we did something very similar with this idea of, you know, this sort of triangle of awareness, engagement, conversion. That was, that was an, that's, that was emergent. That was an emergent distillation of a much of a lot of really complicated things. And that's a lot of what you're communicating in your emails is another marketing is, is bringing this complexity down to some simplicity. That's enough that somebody can understand it and begin to frame their world around that and that that gives them some value. So I feel like we're going off the reservation here. So let's move on. The next question was from Karma about bridge emails. We did, we, we answered this in. We answered this in the, this week's workshop content and we're going to update the bridge email module. The very short answer here, differences between hard bridge and soft bridge. Almost all the bridge emails you create are going to be hard bridges. The exception, a soft bridge is an exception where somebody raises his or her hand before opt in and indicates interest in a product or service or something. So you're getting the active intent early, but you send them through a hybrid remit relationship building series that has a soft bridge that says, and, you know, and now it's not, but this isn't the language, but you are going relationship building a transition with a soft bridge to a story powered promotion. All in sort of one sequence that soft bridge works because someone has already told you at opt in that they're interested in a product or a service or something to purchase. Everything else is a hard bridge and there are two types of hard bridges. There's a solo bridge and an embedded bridge. A solo bridge is a standalone email like the one that was sent for the workshop and one that was sent to everyone, to everyone who didn't own the art of email about the art of email and the workshop. And it was a stand, there are standalone emails that are like, Hey, this is going to happen if you're interested, raise your hand. That's a solo bridge. An embedded bridge is when you have the interest list link embedded into something else like a value newsletter, for example, and we'll write all of this up and put it together. Anything to add to that, Andre, before we jump into your story path thing. Yeah, let me just mention two, two nuances that may not be obvious. And again, we'll clarify this in the module. So the one is the one use case that Sean's just articulated is you go from a relationship building, there's a soft bridge, the transition, and then there's a story powered promotion. And it feels from the person going through it as a single sequence. There's no, there's no interruption. But the way that you create these things, they're all still separate. There's an RBS, it's a separate sequence. The very last email is that soft bridge component. And you know, you at the end of that, you then trigger the story powered promotion through the automation. So they don't, they don't, you know, feel that it just happens. And then through your story powered promotion, it then sends them whichever one. If it's the first time they they enter in your world, it'll obviously send the first story powered promotion that you have in your stack. If you have multiple in a stack, it'll send them the first one, which is why it's good to when you create these things, you will create the first story powered promotion that you know, people are going to get that one first. You know, you you will you will craft that one in in such a way that it's the first promotion you would want somebody to be exposed to, as opposed to the second and fourth and or third time. Someone's at that point, they've really been through one and or two or, you know, or two. And so there's some context there and those can can be different. So that's just the one you answer. And then the other one is with a with a solo hard bridge. So you send out a solo email to to everybody and you asking people to raise their hand if they're interested for something. It's important that that email, certainly if it's going to everybody like a V&L, does it needs to be valuable. It needs to be broadly valuable at the same time. You know, it's it's calling out people that are interested in in this thing behind the click, the raise your hand click. So that needs to be explicit that when they click this thing to raise their hand, they know what's going to happen after it. But at the same time, that that email itself needs to needs to be valuable to those people that aren't going to click that link. So it's not just like a promotion, a single promotion email that goes to everybody that's not really valuable. So I think that's those are the two little nuances that maybe aren't obvious. And Rich actually asked, looks like he asked three questions. But I think what I'll do is I'm just a minute asked earlier about dropping the questions in chat. So I'm just going to drop all of this chat and then I'll sort of pick it apart one at a time. OK, so from Rich about the bridge email you wrote, this is the most effective framework to identify the essential needs of an audience and match those needs precisely to the product or services you offer. So first question, is there a feedback loop in here to really nail identification and matching their needs? Or do you mean that by raising or not raising their hands only? I'll jump in here. Have you jumped in? So the feedback loop is hand raising. Like that's that's what lets us know. Now, of course, that's the first of many pieces of information. So if you think about you have a bridge email to a PLS. So we can use the RTV email for an example as an example. We had two bridge emails, two separate audience segments, two PLS is running. So we have we have lots of different pieces of data. We have a piece of data that says people who already own the art of email. At this particular point in time, X number of them were interested in a live format workshop. OK, that's a piece of data. Then we have additional data for that. And of those people who raised their hand and said they were interested, X number actually were interested enough to purchase. And then we could continue to look at how many people attended the various Q&A calls. We can look at the number of comments we get, the types of questions. There's a richness of data. Yes. And the bridge email sets all of that up. But the bridge email is for the entry point of that data. And we could do the same thing for the other segment. So how many people on our list who did not own the art of email said they were interested in it. And then of those people, how many actually were interested enough to purchase and then how many people to purchase were also interested in the in the workshop. Like, that's all rich data to have. The feedback loop, though, here's the here's the challenge with interpreting this as a feedback loop beyond some really broad strokes, which is this idea that we never step in the same river twice. So we're never going to be able to replicate that simply because context has changed. You know, the point in time we did this is often very true of the PLS. The point in time we did this. Cannot be replicated. Right there. This was a there was some the pent up demand for what was thought to be air and for, which we've now changed to the art of email. There's a brand new course. They're like all of those factors. Come they coalesce around a singular event. Now in the future, if it's not a PLS, if it's a story powered promotion, there, yes, there can be a feedback loop in there that we can look at where, you know, what's for the sake of argument, say on email seven in our emergent marketing onboarding series, our relationship building series becomes a choose your own adventure. We could start to assess how many people what first of all, how many people choose the adventure next at all? And then what adventure do they choose? So do they choose, you know, awareness, engagement or conversion? Like we could look at all of that data and try to. But then it gets to this, like, I think Seth Godin called it like something around basically everything becomes a porn site eventually, where you like so hyper optimized for every single tiny variable and you're moving stuff around. And I don't know that there's a lot of value in that into trying to make like, OK, that's hard. This one email, we get a certain response and well, what if we wanted to increase that response? Well, at what point are we are we messing things up versus making a full system available to an audience and letting the audience find their way through that system? Knowing that 80, 85 percent of the time, they're just going to be engaging with content and they'll have the ability to raise their hand when they may be interested in buying things. I think that's really the difference here and not the temptation to gain the system to try to squeeze a few more cells out of it. Hey, that that that scares me a little bit. Any thoughts on that? Andre, I don't even know if I'm answering the question in the right direction at this point. I'm still just hurt about your handwriting comments. Yeah, and they haven't seen a handwriting. It's so I have nothing wrong with my handwriting. So, you know, there's one of the one of the most important elements. And this is kind of implied, perhaps, but it's the quality of the signal that we get. So it has been very intentional about so if you're sitting out at a hard bridge, you know, whether it's part of a newsletter or let's just keep this simple and it's a solo hard bridge. It's a single email that's that's that's going out. And let's just make it even simpler. It's going out to everybody on the email list. That's that's outside of an obvious. So everybody available gets this gets this bridge email. Well, it's the quality of framing that hand raise that is probably the most important factor at that point in time. And again, you know, as Sean said, if you had to send out that identical email one month earlier or three months later, that signal you get back will be different because people people are in different places. So it's you can only we're in this point in time. So you can only, you know, when you decide to send something out, that's when you send it out. And then it's the quality of framing that that in the most precise way that the people that are raising their hand, they have, you know, they as we articulate in the bridge email module, there's this there's this metaphorical idea of, you know, Payne's gains and jobs to be done. And we want those signals to be really hard. You know, everybody's thinking about this, you know, inside of them, their heads differently. You know, some people are trying to move away from from from gain. I mean, from Payne and others are want to move towards pleasure. But so framing that in such a way that you can try and tease out or the sevens, eights, nines and tens, those people and everybody else, we don't want them to raise their hand. So that's more important. And obviously, that's a feedback loop. But it's not about trying to get as many clicks as possible. So people know that you don't have to share the price. That's that's just something. Yeah. So I don't know that answers the question, but I think that's the effect I talked for 10 minutes. I don't think I answered the question at all. I'm going to move on to the next question. It's funny. This next question is something that I've been having a conversation about this with one of our mutual friends, Aaron, for months. And yesterday, normally I would have had a conversation sort of like checking with him every week. Normally I would have had this conversation tomorrow, but I'll be driving to Boston tomorrow. So I had the conversation yesterday and it's really it's the answer to the question Rich asked that I wouldn't have been. I would have given a much different answer to it. I think a much less sophisticated answer to it yesterday. So what a credit Aaron, I can't want to share his full name just for his privacy. But this is there's a lot of Aaron's thinking that is inspired. I see he's brilliant. So here's the question. Do you use the results of these bridge emails, percentage of raised hands, replies, comments to influence your paid ads to build better customers? So there's a short answer here and then there's a much more nuanced answer. The short answer is no, right, for a variety of reasons. Because the systems that we're designing for the paid ads that we use, we do not expect monetization on our paid ads in the short term. And that's that's not true for everybody. I mean, a very typical direct response model looks at three different metrics, right? So it's cost to acquire a customer. And then average order value because there's that very first sale. So that's where we get this distinction between front and back. And there's a front end sale designed specifically to offset or eliminate the cost to acquire a customer. And then there's LTV, which is lifetime value. And how you measure that, like the time frames that you choose for those things, dramatically influences how the business operates. So many of the large direct response companies that you've heard of before, particularly many in the financial newsletter space, their business model really is around this idea of recouping approximately 80% of the cost to acquire the customer over the first 30 days. And then making their money over the six months. Now, that's just that's a business model, but it creates so many downstream effects. Our business model is because it's so wildly different, doesn't create, it creates very different effects. So our business model is we're not trying to monetize. We're not trying to offset the cost to acquire a customer in the first 30 days. That's the first change. And I'm mentioning this because if you don't understand that distinction, then it would be very easy to just, number one, dismiss what we're talking about and say that doesn't make any sense. Because it doesn't make sense in a context where you need to recoup the cost to acquire a customer in 30 days. Our model doesn't make sense for that. But what it does make sense for, and this is a very important point, what does it make sense for? And this is really the larger insight that I think is worth sharing here. That when we look at the sort of four decades, more than four decades of experience, and we look at lots of insights from other people like Dean Jackson and others who have really looked at how sales tend to graph over time and where sales happen and the nature of most markets. When we look at all of that information, what it really distills down to is this trust that if we don't screw things up, right, so if we don't do something wildly stupid, if we focus on building relationships for the majority of the time that we're interacting with an audience, and then we let our audience raise their hand at opportunities to monetize the relationship. If we do those three things, we believe that the net result is going to be revenue positive, significantly revenue positive, not within 30 days, very unlikely. We'll get a pretty strong signal within 90 days, right? The 15 or 20-ish, whatever the small, the minority of people are going to buy are likely to buy in that first window. They're going to buy, we're going to get some kind of signal. And we are pretty confident that that signal that we're going to get is going to far offset the cost to acquire those customers, but we don't get wound up about it. But what we believe in more broadly is that we've created this system that we can comfortably pay to add people to because the system is going to produce a series of results, one of which is financial. And if we just focus on those sort of three principles, which I think are irreducible, and this is the conversation Aaron and I have been having really where we got to yesterday. The first one being don't screw things up. So don't, in a million ways, to screw things up to not do what you say you're going to do, to push for the sale really hard, there are lots of ways to screw things up. So don't screw things up. Don't go after the wrong audiences, all that stuff. So don't screw things up. Emphasize building the relationships for the majority of the time that you're communicating with your audience. Overinvest in relationships. And then create opportunities to monetize those relationships. But where the other person is raising their hand to show interest. If we do those three things, we're confident that the result is going to be positive in financial terms. So we're willing to invest money in that. That's a long way of, that's a very long no to this question. Because I think if we, if we try to do what this question suggests, which is, you know, we, first of all, we would have to, we would have to change things pretty quickly, right? We would, it would, it would, this decision to use percentages of raised hands and replies and comments to influence paid ads. We would have to shorten the cycle of relationship building early because we, we would, there would be too much distance between the ad and the, you know, when somebody raised the hand. So immediately we would start making decisions that are counter to the larger decisions that were, that are foundational for us. And to be clear, you know, we're not investing, you know, $15 million a month in traffic generation. And if you're doing that, then, you know, or some really significant amount of money, you have a direct response model and you need to, to recoup 80, 100% of that cost to acquire a customer to keep the ball rolling every month, then the model we're suggesting doesn't make sense. But conversely, this idea that somehow the direct response model is the best model for everyone, that doesn't make sense. Because that model has requirements. And this is the thing that's unspoken. I don't want to go too far on a tangent here, but this is just going to be a chance to rant for a second here. I didn't think I was going to rant at all today because I felt super calm when I woke up, but now I want to rant. So here's my rant. There, there's nothing wrong with the direct response model at all. What's wrong with it is this idea that it's the only model that everybody should use. So, you know, 20 years ago when we were all sort of fumbling around trying to figure out like what was, what was digital marketing going to be? And no one really knew. And then, and then it was sort of this battle between the brand crowd and the direct response crowd and the direct response, both of them sort of gone different directions. And then there are some hybrids and other things. But really, the direct response model became the model that a lot of the quote unquote gurus began to promote. And that has almost become the default model. Like this is sort of this underlying assumption that, of course, you have to do it that way for all of these reasons. But what no one ever shares, I say no one, which is rarely shared, are two things. One, that model works for a small segment of the larger audience, the period. That's just, that's reality. That's gravity. And two, there are requirements to do that model well, one of which is to have a lot of money to invest in traffic acquisition. Right. You can, if you, if you can make the numbers work with a 2% conversion rate and still make your customer acquisition in your AOV work, like that's fine. But to do that, you're going to need to be spending tens, hundreds of thousands, if not millions of dollars on traffic acquisition to make that work. And most of the people in our audience aren't spending that level of money on traffic acquisition and don't want to take the risk on to do that. So when you're unwilling to do the two or three things that are crucial to make a model work, don't choose that model. That's the alpha and the omega. Period. So don't just be aware of that in your decision making. So this is a, I feel like this is a very long, succulent answer around this question, but there's, there's a really powerful question within Rich's question about the, the, how we use things that happen later to go back and make decisions about how we acquired our customer or how we acquired our prospects. Right. And what we're suggesting is if you build the system like we described in the art of email, the system is so damn good. And it's so responsive to people that you can comfortably add customers to it or add prospects to it, knowing it's going to produce a financially positive result. Now that doesn't mean go out and sell the farm and dump all of your money in it and cross your fingers. But it means that because this is a system that you're iterating over time and that you're building upon the feedback of your audience and in seeing, like for example, a PLS that works really well, but then you start taking some of the ingredients and you make it into a story powered promotion when it's evergreen, you're building everywhere on successes and the system begins to do something that no part of that system can do on its own. And this would seem like a source of customer modeling. Is any of that right? Um, I'll jump in and then I think we'll probably ping pong on this one, but I sort of have a thought to share. Um, I think the bridge email is, well, let me take one quick step back. Um, a lot of what we did, we, meaning Andre and I over the last, I don't know, eight, nine, 10 months, um, was, was really take Andre's ideas of it. Sort of the core ideas that Andre has contributed to the world of email marketing. And then I've used those with clients, but there was a lot of sort of this back and forth, um, of me, you know, I just, I, I seek to understand things. So there was a lot of me asking questions. And then when I, when Andre would answer the questions, um, I would, I would sort of mentally model the downstream consequences and then I would go back and say like, okay, well, if that, if what you're describing here is, is true, then these implications would be true. And, and how does that fit into the overall system? So there's a lot of, of really, and then, and often we change things to be clear, um, that we would kind of go back and say, okay, there is a better way to explain this and we were, it's, it was this constant, like smashing it together, tearing it apart, smashing the new thing together, tearing it apart to get to something that feels irreducible, right? Like that you need all of it and, and you, and there aren't parts of it that you, you should remove that aren't going to have really significant effects. Like we want this to be then sort of Einstein's idea as simple as possible, but no simpler. Um, with all of that as context, the, the bridge email for me is the thing that I think makes the whole thing work. And what, by that, what I mean is it does these two things really well. It, it rewards attention for the people who are the sevens, three times. Um, and it protects inattention for the people who aren't in that point of time. And of those two things, I don't know that one is more important than the other, but I do think that the consequences of protecting inattention are profound because we've all experienced being on somebody's email list where it's just too much, right? And we stopped paying attention at some point and, and we're not engaged. Our attention is finite in many ways. Um, and one of the ways I think our attention is finite is that when we're, when we're engaging with a certain topic or a certain, um, you know, idea and market, whatever it is that our interest ebbs and flows. And when we're interested, we're interested. And when we're not, we're largely not, I think the bridge email models that so well, and it prevents this thing from happening where it's like, Hey, just, you know, people can just ignore me if they're not interested in the promotion right now, and I'll just send it to everybody. But what you're basically training people to do is to learn how to ignore you. Right. That's, that to me, that's the worst thing in the world. We would ever want to train our audience to do. And, and with the bridge email, I think unlock that I believe is it's super power that really makes the entire system work as well as it does is that by, by not training people to ignore your messaging when they're not interested, it has the effect of training them to pay attention to your messaging because it's, it's less, right? They're not getting everything that you're doing. And what they are seeing, they're seeing in response to what they're interested in in that moment when they're raising their hand. And the consequences of that single decision, that single mechanism, I think reverberate throughout all of the art of email. If you, if the way I think about things like this one, sort of like a theory of constraints or just a basic causality logic model is if what would happen if we removed any piece of the system, would the system be better or worse? Would it fundamentally alter the system? So if we look, if we just assume for the sake of argument that we have a robust art of email inspired system and it has every part. So people come in, there's a relationship building series, either for a particular product or in general, there's a consistent value newsletter. We have soap opera series that sort of set things up and then bridge emails connect anything where someone needs to raise their hand to show interest, whether or not it's a soap opera series, which is primarily educational or a promotional series, a story, power promotion or PLS, which is educational and transactional. Like that gatekeeper, if we have all of those things, can we, what could we remove and still have the same system? And to be clear, I do think in different scenarios, there are things that some people can remove. If you have an enrollment only, like a time bound, a cohort based enrollment system, I think you can get rid of story, power, promotions. You can always, you can just do a PLS each time. So yes, that's quote unquote optional. But one thing that clearly is not optional in the art of emails, if you take out the bridge email, the entire system falls apart. It stops working. I mean, it'll work for a bit. It will work, but it will not work the way it should work or at the level that it could work. So the centrality of the bridge email, it is so damn important that wrapping your head around how it becomes the gatekeeper is so incredibly important. And to see that as the thing that's always, the way I imagine it for our audience is, I know we can do whatever we want and really go overboard and communicate with our audience. We can send lots of emails. We can talk about our stuff when it's time to talk about it. We can sell when it's time to sell. All of that I can be completely comfortable with because I know a mechanism exists to protect everybody who's not interested right now from seeing any of that. And I never have to feel like, you know, we got to be careful. The people who aren't really interested, we want to give them a little something. It's like, no, I don't have to worry about that. And it's because the people who are reading what you're writing in your emails are almost always actively interested in it. And yes, they may get a newsletter once in a while, everybody gets them and they're not paying attention and that's fine. You're not, that's not creating too much cognitive load. It's not bothering them. I just, I can't emphasize this enough. The importance of the, the central importance of the big, of the bridge email. So when, you know, Rich says, it feels like this is a big inflection point. And, you know, you're not sure if you're looking at too much. You can't, you can't think too much about the bridge email. It's that important. All right, there's your setup, Andre. Run with it. Make that sound smart. I've got nothing. Yeah, I'll just, I'll just add the fact that, you know, the whole idea of the bridge email was, was born from the idea of modeling and rewarding behavior. So subscribe to behavior. And the only way to do that is by framing something in such a way that it pulls someone's attention forward. And the only way to do that is by, you know, obviously framing and context. And then, you know, having a mechanism in there that allows people to raise their hand, you know, this, this idea of the bridge email is, is, is more refined. You know, it's, I think it's easier to wrap your head around this idea of a bridge email than when I first started to do, or respond to madness, where it was just really this, this, this, this me trying to model behavior. And the only way to do that is to, you know, and, and, and recognizing the fact that at any point in time, any subscriber, we're not going to have their attention. That's just impossible. You know, if you've, if you've got a hundred people adding themselves to an email list every single day, well, six months out, that doesn't mean that a hundred percent of subscribers are going to be interested in whatever you got to say. You know, that's, I'm going to use that, that word again, a, a silisco, you know, but it's, it's this oscillation of, of attention from, you know, hard to low to I'm interested to, I'm not interested for a million different reasons because we operating in this, in this chaotic system of laugh. And, you know, we just operate our little businesses within that. So there's these, there's these external factors at play all the time. And it influences and it affects people's behavior. You know, they've lost the job. They've, they having a divorce, you know, things are happening that affect stuff that we're thinking that we have control over and we don't. So these bridge emails or these, these mechanisms to ask people to raise their hand is, it's, it's, it's there to, to, to reward, well, to put out attention and then obviously reward attention. And those people that just aren't paying attention, there's no need to pit them over the head and try and try, try versions of blood force, force trauma to try and get their attention. You know, they just, we don't have the attention at this point in time and maybe next month or next week or tomorrow it'll be different. So, yeah, I think you're right in the fact that the idea behind the bridge email makes us all, you know, you can't take that away because then you have a system that's not responsive to, to, to subscribe, subscribe behavior. It's just a linear thing. And, you know, we're all on many email lists and one of the, you know, broadly speaking, most email lists are the way that people interact with their, with their subscribers is, it's all very linear. It's broadcast emails are going out all the time and whether it's a promotion or a, or a, or a newsletter or whatever, it's just, you know, people, it's just it, it, they, these emails go to everybody all the time. There's no, there's no nuance inside of it. And if you're not paying attention, you just don't read the, read the emails. But then, as Sean said, there's, you know, you can train people to just mentally opt out. So there may still be on the, on the email list, but they're not paying attention forever. So, you know, this, you know, understanding the idea behind the bridge email is fundamental to, to creating a system that's going to respond in a way that's just going to be amazing. So that's anything I'll add. And it's interesting. Like it's interesting to surface assumptions. I think this is, this is right up there with the power of questions. Right. So the ability to surface assumptions and to look at them in the cold light of day and say, is this true? But it's such a critical scale and very, it's very uncommon. But here, as you were talking, I was thinking about one of the hidden assumptions in the conventional email marketing. So most, if you ask, if you wind up a hundred people, you have some, you know, reasonable amount of experience with email marketing and say, Hey, do you send all of your offers to everyone? And most of them overwhelming, they're going to say yes. And then she say, okay, why do you do that? Right. Like what, why would you do that? A simple question. Most people are going to say some variation of, of belief that there's no cost to doing that because, I mean, first of all, there's no extra cost. This is one of the things that you hear all the time, said about email, there's, you know, it doesn't cost to send more emails. So why not send more emails, which is just so ludicrous. It's not worth addressing. There's no financial cost. But if, but the assumption that there's, that you can just send things to everybody all the time and that there's no cost to that. So why not do that? Right. There's no downside. That's such a flawed assumption. Of course there's a downside because every time you send some, something to somebody who's not interested in that something, there's, they move just a tiny bit or sometimes depending on the context, a significant way is away from you. Of course there's a cost to doing that. So the assumption that we can just send any time to everybody and the people who will respond will respond and everybody else, there's no downside. It's so fundamentally ludicrous to think of, think of things that way. And once you externalize, once you draw attention to the fact that there is a cost for doing that, well, then you make different decisions. And that's really a lot of what we're talking about here is we're just, we're drawing attention to the reality of the situation in, in every level. Right. There's a, there's an underlying reality around how people, general behave when they purchase, people who are going to purchase, how they actually purchase. There's a, that can be modeled. So why not adapt systems to based on the reality of that? But there's also other things, other parts of this reality that we need to adapt to. One of which is there is a cost of, there's a, there's a, attention has a quality to it and there's, attention can be diminished incrementally when we continue to show things to people they're not interested in. That's human nature. So by not doing it, that's one of the, you know, what I mentioned before, don't do dumb shit, like don't make these dumb mistakes. That's a dumb mistake to just assume everybody wants to see your stuff all the time. It's an easily correctable dumb mistake. And the, the mechanism to correct it is to use bridge emails. That's, that's a short answer to the question. All right. Let's say we have some non-module specific questions. So this will be interesting. This is Ricardo. I will drop this into chat too. All right. Here's the question. Or the, I'll, I think I'll probably read, I'll read through the second question. Okay. In the case that you have a premium or high ticket offer and are running ads to a typical application funnel, how would the non-linear world look? Um, I don't have a good answer that's connected to, to paid. Um, my answer will be closer to what you were saying early on about direct response versus versus the different response. Um, uh, a different approach. So. I mean, yeah, I mean, I, I, I, I sense an answer in this one, this, which will turn into a rant. So why not? The rant door. So why not? Why not keep ranting? Oh, there, there's a presupposition. Are there, I don't know if it's a presupposition in this. And this is so common. So Ricardo, I'm not signaling you out, but this is such a beautiful example of a presupposition, which is, and we mentioned this, I think on the last call. And I, I, I want to have an opportunity to just mention this over and over and over again. The idea that most people who are likely to buy from you over, over the people who are going to buy over a two year period, the tiny minority of them are going to buy in the first 90 days is, it is gravity. We were, it's not something we made up. It is the reality of the situation. So when you create a, a, a, and this is very typical. When you create a funnel approach that's add to landing page, the VSL, the application to call, you are creating a system that is only going to get you a small minority of people. Period. It doesn't mean it's good or bad. It just means you're limiting your business to a tiny slice of the market. And if in the more you optimize to get more of that tiny slice of the market, first of all, there's an upper end to it. Right. Second of all, you always run the danger of pissing off people who would otherwise buy from you later. Like that's a downstream consequence. You can't, and I love this idea of like, can you armify like something that is contrary to the foundational principles of the art of email? I mean, sure, I think you can get a little incremental gain from it. The better thing to do here is to, to think differently about what you're doing. Now, of course, Ricardo was introduced to a really important point here, which is they're spending a lot of money on ads. So they can't just, you know, throw caution to the winds. Oh, you know, we're going to rebuild the system entirely, cross our fingers and hope that it all works. Like I get that. However, the money that you're spending on ads, the signal that you're getting from the funnel that you've created, from the entire approach, everything that every result you get is a downstream consequences of the decisions that you're making of how to get that result. Period. That is inescapable. So to try to do the thing that's suboptimal better is the upside to that is very, very low. Right. You might mean, and again, I don't know what you're looking for for upside. So maybe you can get it 20% better. And maybe 20% better makes a huge difference. If the numbers are right, sure. But the reality is to when you send people through that model, you're, you're going to create a certain type of prospect and you're, you're not going to get the bulk of the people who might otherwise buy your high ticket off. And then what I think I would do after I built the parallel system is I would then look to the system that you already have. And I would use behaviorally based retargeting to figure out where people drop out of the system that already exists to drop them into the other system instead. So people who go from ad to landing page and don't convert, don't opt in. Now I might retarget them to the other landing page with different messaging or and or people who get to the VSL page and don't proceed past that. Like you just lost them there. I would have that perhaps retarget to the other, the other model. Just being on the idea that this is an assumption and I don't know if this is true. You would have to test this that the people who didn't, some significant percentage of the people, there's sort of two assumptions here. Assumption number one is the nature of the system you've created already is attracting a subset of the audience, not the full audience. So having a parallel system that attracts a broader audience, that's one assumption. May or may not be true, but I would be willing to bet it's true. The second assumption that I'm making is that some people who get sucked into the system that you've already created are not ideal for that particular methodology and that if you got them out of that approach and into a slower, more relationship focused approach that you would get additional value from. You get additional value because you've sort of missed with one segment of the audience that you're already getting. So that's how I would approach this question. Of course, Andre and I have a little conversation to have later when we have to work through all of our all the things that we've just exposed to everyone here. All right. Here's the question. What are your thoughts on the strategy? I don't know why strategies and quotes here. So I'm curious now. What are your thoughts on the strategy of running front end and back end offers? As you know it very well, with a typical funnel approach, marketers who try to break even or even lose some money initially to get a customer using a low cost offer and then sell a more expensive product later as the back end, since funnel is no longer something we need to worry about. Should this front end back end strategy be thrown out the window as well? I'll jump on this one first. There's no helmet. Why? Right? I mean, front end, back end, those distinctions. I mean, they're consider that a spectrum. What's a front end offer versus a back end offer? I mean, I guess there is one one clear definition of front end offer is the thing that you show people first, often associated with paid traffic, often to monetize the cost of paid traffic and to lower the threshold to purchase based on a false assumption, incidentally, which will externalize in a second. But based on the false assumption that a customer is always all things being equal to customers, always more valuable than prospect over the long term. And that once you can get customers for free, right, this is direct response one to one, once you can get customers for free or close to free, meaning that the cost to acquire the customers offset by the initial average order value of that first front end offer, everything else. It's just it's the gravy train, right? At the back end, you're at all the time, the bittin' of monies in the back end. Right? OK. Sure. There's truth in all of this. But but but but there are some assumptions in here too. So here's the assumption, assumption number one that's not true. We've written about this. Andres destroyed this very well not too long ago in one of our VNLs, which is this idea that somehow a seven dollar customer is is going to buy a seven hundred dollar product is there's this that's such it's so stupid. It's almost I almost don't want to say it out loud, right? So the idea is that if somebody buys anything, they're more valuable than someone who doesn't. OK, maybe more valuable means the likelihood of them buying something else. But that's so that that mindset is so easily undermined. And you know, there's the internet is littered with free plus shipping book offers that disprove this idea, right? You think the free plus shipping book offer say 1095, 995, 1095 price point and bump offer to 20 bucks upsell one upsell to 50 to maybe 100 to 250. Right. And you try to get your cart value up to getting customers for free. And then you're trying to sell them a two thousand dollar offer later. And this idea that if you can just get people to take out their credit card for 10 bucks, that they're infinitely more valuable when it comes time to sell on something for two thousand, that's it's bullshit. Right. It's it's it's not that's not the reason to do it. The reason to do it is to get a bunch of customers for free. Right. And to get to build a list for free. That's that's the way to do it. And there's sure. Right. There's there's no reason not to just recognize that the people you're getting with that strategy are a small segment of the audience. There are the 15 to 20 percent who will buy fast. And you can build an entire business around those 15 to 20 percent. But you can't build you can't build the same business to get the other 80 percent. That's that that's just a fact. So it's not back end versus front end. It's what do you do in the beginning? And does that preclude you from getting most of the people who would be your customers if you did something differently on the front end? And it doesn't again, it doesn't have to be either or right. You can in in in ClickFunnels is built on this idea incidentally, which is two step opt in. If you ever bought anything through a ClickFunnels funnel, what does it do? You go to buy something and if you don't buy it, it's a two step process to buy. The first step is lead gen. Right. You put in your info and you click like next or you go on to the purchase thing. Well, that that's lead capture. And it's not obvious that it's lead capture incidentally. And then if you're like, you know what, I don't want to buy this. What the hell was I thinking you take off? Well, now you're a lead. They still you're still value. So now you have this sort of three these are three prongs or you've got people who didn't buy people who bought the low cost offer and then people who bought the low cost offer plus other stuff and you hope all of that blends together. But that that attracting people with a front end offer is it's attractive to a small segment of your audience. Right. So when you make that front end back end, you get all the benefits of customers for free, essentially, if you do it right, if you're offset, you're cost to acquire a customer with your average order value, then you can build a big list for free, essentially, and you have to put whatever money comes in. And then, you know, you hope that that evens out or close to it. But that that that has consequences and the alternative. And again, there's no it this isn't right or wrong. It just creates downstream effects and you you're not going to get the majority of the audience with that approach. And you just that's I don't know any other way to say it. So having a back end more expensive in a back end. A back end generally is thought to be more expensive, but it doesn't have to be. You can have a series of back end offers, right? Build a relationship with an audience over time. That's what we do. We have our price plan, our highest price plan is four ninety five. And is that a back end? Well, we don't have a front end. So it's not a back end. It's just an end. It's not even an end to beginning. Right. So when we make these artificial buckets, I think that what I'm trying to get to here, which is taking me so long to get here, when we when we make when we categorize things, those categories have consequences. And every day we sit down to a buffet of consequences. So when you make it a when you embrace front end, a front end to offset the cost of paid traffic and make your money LTV on back end, there's nothing wrong with that. It just has consequences, both good and bad, good consequence. You can often build a business faster because you're putting money in and getting money out, you can invest more money in and get more money out. And so that there's plenty of benefit to that. But there are also some downstream consequences that if you run a business like that only, you're missing out on the majority. What makes that business work, that strategy work really well, comes at a cost of not being attracted to the majority of people who would eventually buy that same offer from you over a longer period of time. And you just need to be aware of that and that decision making, because you can't get that strategy to do both things. You need a different strategy to get the totality of the marketplace. I think that's my that's my not so simple answer to be forever to get there. All right. What do you think about funnels front end, back end? Yeah, I think I'll use a product that we that we don't actually have yet to to maybe put on a certain thread here that that, you know, so I think for us, we don't necessarily, you know, we don't quote, have a have a back end, as Sean said. But at some point when we finally release TLB Lab, we might make a decision at that point that we only going to offer TLB Lab to our audience. So it's not available on the website to to somebody that doesn't really know us because it's a it's a type of product that is really for people that that know us and the way that we operate and want to get a, you know, hear us think out loud. So it'll be a mechanism for, you know, for people to hear our thoughts and be the part of how we think about things. And that's that's not really relevant to somebody that doesn't know us. So we at that point when we finally do do that, that that product may not exist on the website at all. It'll only be that offer will only be to to people on our email list. But then it might even be narrower than that. We might make a decision that we only going to offer it to customers. So a customer of one or more products, only they will will be people that will get the bridge email to raise their hand to be part of TLB Lab, maybe. So then that in both of those cases, it will be a back end for us. But that's not because it's a back end. It's I mean, it's it's more a decision because the type of customer that that's going to be relevant for we don't we don't want people to join TLB Lab and then unsubscribe one month or two months later. We want people to come and love it so much that they stay on for an entire series or a year or whatever that is. That's what we want. So how do we make that happen? Well, probably only exposing it to either a portion of our audience or our email audience that already knows and then it's that it's not available on the websites. I suppose in that context, we could have a back end, but maybe that's that's just a better way of thinking about front ends and back ends. That's it. So I've got one of the next question here. So an agency question. So I'm scared to even look at it. All right. This is a great question. Well, I mean, I ran an agency for 22 years. So how would I use the modern art of email? And I had that same model. So I charge clients monthly retainer. What wouldn't I implement in if I if I were setting if I were if I decided today that after revealing the depths of my soul in this call to Andre, that we were parting ways because of the how Rudy is about my emails and the fact that we argue all the time, which you now understand that we're just going to part ways. I'm going to go back and open an agency again, which for the record, I'm never going to do it, not even if Jesus Christ himself showed up and asked me to do it. So how would I use the art of email? Would I use a relationship building series? Yes. What I think my audience would likely read it? Yes. I think it would be the I would like to use that. I would I would create an art of email relationship building series around this idea of how to make an ideal customer. And I would really try to get rid of the people who are not going to be ideal customers. And I have a short list of customer avatars who annoy the hell out of me. And I would make sure that I called out every one of those avatars in my relationship building series and begged and pleaded with them not to not just to just please unsubscribe and never bother me again, because I'm so jaded and working with certain types of clients. So yes, I would use a relationship building series. Would I use soap opera series? Yes, I would use. But my my offerings, let me actually ask a better question. What would I do after a relationship building series? We pay traffic primarily and then some more consultative strategy stuff. So I would have I would create those first as entry points, because I would want to make as much as I'd want to make good customers in those series, I would really actually be using those as screening tools to to deliberately inject red flags to get people to to bail who want the right. Now we just call them out on the behaviors. And just my way of example, so people can understand what kind of behavior. The thing that irritates me most in the world as a former service provider are people who would hire me because of my expertise and tell me how to do my work or argue with me about how I did my work when, in fact, the decisions we were making, I forgot more about what we were talking about in the moments before that meeting than that the client would ever know in their entire lifetime. So that was not a real big one for me. I didn't love that one. And I would call that out. I have a good story. He could tell people what he wanted them to accomplish or he could tell them what he wanted them to do. He couldn't do both. So I would have that in a relationship building series and get people to self select. Right. Like if you really want to be able to be a hands on manager with your service provider, I am not the person for you and it will not work. And I just want you to know that up front. So I would make relationship building series that way. And I would probably overemphasize that. I would probably make them longer. And I would not want I would not give somebody the option. I mean, they could they certainly could pick up the phone. And I would not encourage somebody to try to become a customer until they had gone through that screening series. Right. Because I would want that to really I would want that to be an educational tool for them to know how to be a customer that I could work with well. You may have a different approach to this, but I'm just thinking a lot of how I would do that. After that, what would I do next? Obviously, bridging emails would protect everything. I think I would I would get most value from a combination of relationship building series and story powered promotions. And I would I do I would do relationship building series first. I would do customer onboarding series next. That's something that I had a real blind spot for. And it would just be I would to walk them through what to expect. Because I often repeated myself. You know, things like, you know, this is this is very typical for a service provider. This is very typical for me, especially when I was doing a lot of Google ads, I would come in and within the first 30 days, often increase results, whatever was important by singular double digits and then decrease ad spend simultaneously by at least 50 percent because so much Google ads is waste. So that would happen immediately. And of course, the client thinks I'm, you know, in those scenarios, like they think two things, one over, which is, wow, he's a genius and two, not so over, which is every month is going to be like this. Well, you can't do it every month. So to have a customer onboarding series that match the inflection points of a service provider relationship and to pre frame what was going to be happening next in the relationship in a positive way, rather than what I found myself doing a lot was trying to explain it after the fact, usually irritated and wondering why they didn't understand it themselves. So rather than saying, you know, in month three, when the client is trying to say, like, wow, you know, our improvement this month isn't nearly like it was month one and me bashing my head against a wall and say, explain to me how that would be possible mathematically, like instead of doing that, it would have been better with a customer onboarding series in month two to start setting up and framing this idea that, of course, the thing that the improvements are going to diminish over time the scale because we're we're bringing and I could frame it with some sort of metaphor that we're bringing that these these accounts or these campaigns into this tighter focus and what we're going to do once we get that firing rate and running really well is when we start to layer on new campaigns when we fix what already exists. But we're not going to get the benefits out of the first campaign at a certain point, like I could have framed that in a way that would have prevented problems down the line. So again, thinking out loud, relationship building series first, customer onboarding series shortly after I might actually do those in parallel. No, sorry, in tandem. First, by order of priority of my business. So, you know, paid traffic would have been by far the biggest. From Karma, do you think PLS is this product series or more powerful and do better than story power promotions? Wonder if they are due to the nature of their time. But that's a great question we can answer next week when we talk about PLS. We can just answer it now. I think we have I think Andre and I have different opinions on this. We may maybe it's we'll just answer it now. And then next week we'll answer it again and see if our opinions are closer together. I think a PLS in general is going to be more powerful than a story powered promotion simply because it has built in urgency that closes. And I think the consequence of the fact that it ends and they have to make a decision is always going to be if we're defining powerful as increasing likelihood to buy in a certain period of time by definition, it has to be more powerful. All things being equal and all things are never equal. But that's my my thought on it. Andre, your thought, SPP versus PLS. Yeah, I mean, I don't think they they two things that need to go into a ring and spiral out because both of them work within the same system to produce results. So one of the each each has pros and cons. And there's a reason why both exist inside of an auto email and supply system. And they both contribute to the revenue engine because everyone's businesses are unique. And I think most people will use a combination of both. You know, the reality is you can't do a PLS every day, you probably can't do one every week. It's unlikely that you can do one every single month. So PLS is all kind of things that you do a few times a year for most people. So that's it. Yes, they can make a lot of money when you when you do them. But on the other hand, the story powered promotion is like a PLS that can run every single day. And it can run every single day because it's it's in a system that is responding to people's behavior and people. So long as you've got lead flow coming in, people are going to be clicking on these links and triggering story powered promotions all the time while you're sleeping every single day. If you have a big enough system and you have enough lead flow, you could certainly be getting sales every single day. And if you look back, the sales are all coming from story powered promotions or you know, various story powered promotions. And you maybe you only do in that context, you're only doing one or two or three PLS's a year. In that case, story powered promotions will outperform a PLS or they'll own if you if you, you know, measuring it over the course of a calendar year. Other other businesses, you know, they just don't know that they either don't have enough lead flow, they don't have enough offers. So it's and it's easier to just do PLS's. Well, then, you know, when you when you look at it over the course of a year, then the PLS's will contribute to more money because maybe there's any one or two story powered promotions and they kind of link to affiliate offers because somebody doesn't have enough offers of the own. Maybe they only got one product. So, you know, in a world where you only have one product, then PLS is really the thing. And SPPs are really going to be wrapped around affiliate promotions and things like that. So it really depends. But I think the point is they both contribute and they can both contribute a lot. And, you know, one's automated and that's and that's beautiful for what it does. And the one's not automated and that's beautiful for what it does. And together, it's it's it's a thing of beauty once you have this thing working well. And if you've got, you know, lead flow coming in and you've have enough time to build out your auto email inspired email system, you know, when you look back, both of those things will have a huge contribution to the amount of money you make. I think I'm going to change my answer. Yeah, I'm going to change my answer. I think my answer is that the question is unanswerable. The reason some of the reasons you you mentioned in other things too. So like if you have a let's say you have a static list with it, doesn't that you're not getting a lot of new people on the list? Every time you do a PLS assembly buys that offer, you diminish the potential for that offer being purchased again, because fewer people own it or more people own it. Right. So there's there's a consequence of the PLS model that is influenced by how you get your traffic. There's there's just too many things that can be like it just can't be it just can't be a situation where all things are being equal. Right. I mean, I guess we could have a scenario where, you know, every day you get 100 new leads and over the course of the new off. But you would never offer the same thing. PLS versus story powered promotion really. So like there's no way to say like over the course of in an ideal world, you would have to answer this question, you would have a PLS, maybe cohort based system running in parallel with a on demand story powered promotion version and you would run say three to four promotions per year. And then the rest of their list would go. They would kind of do it whenever they wanted to. And then at the end of the year, you would tally up how many you sold from the PLS versus the story powered promotion. There's just no way to run those side by side. This is a classic example of the answer is both and the fact that both exist means you model the system to your business and you have more tools to work with. So yeah, I wanted to change my answer. It's always funny to hear what I'm thinking at first and then change my mind midstream. All right. Second question from anonymous. Next one, when you are a really narrow niche, the subject matter itself is pretty small, for example, teaching one particular meditation technique. How do you produce more free content that is not in your course? That's a good question. This gets back to the this is a sort of a marketing idea that has that has been around forever and expanded on. So the first iteration of it is the idea that people don't buy. People don't buy drills because they want drills. They buy drills because they want to make holes. So focus on the hole. And then Seth Godin came along and said, people don't buy drills because they want to make holes. They buy drills because they want to make holes that they want to hang shelves on the wall that they can put pictures of their family because they like how they feel when they see pictures of their family and that they were able to do the work themselves to make that happen. So I think that's a really good model for thinking about giving away free content. Is that you're there's only so much to say about the technique. Don't talk about the technique. Talk about and figure out why the person benefits somebody wants from learning that technique and how their life changes as a result of knowing that technique and what things might lead up to them wanting to know that technique in all the million ways that that cascades throughout their life when they know how to do this thing, like that's where to focus your attention. Not on the thing it's never the thing. It's never and even what what we do is the mechanism, the mechanisms of sending email and all of that. That's that's a vehicle. Right. Like if we woke up tomorrow and there was no email, we would figure out different ways to communicate with audiences, right? Because it's the it's the relationships with the audience. It's all the interactions. It's the the interacting with people to help bring value out into the world. That's what we care about. Like that's everything. It's just an expression of that. So, you know, we're creating free content. It's a lot less about the how to do all of these things. It's the why would we want to do these things and how and like mixing all of those together. So the narrower the niche is on the how side, the more you need to focus on the why and the one side. It's probably a better way to think about it. You think about this one. Yeah, I think that's that's that's great. That's exactly the right way to think about it. You know, it's the paintings on the walls, the shelves, things like that. This is all these and all this amazing stuff by doing something that doesn't scale. And so the VIP email, you know, it's just one expression of that.
