And you can create systems on the back end that do certain things that don't necessarily email somebody. It could email us and then we reach out and do stuff that is part of our feedback loop. That's all I'll say. I think the only thing I would add to this as quickly is that sometimes people do this the opposite direction. And it's kind of it's an interesting mistake, I think. And so you'll often the focus is on the people who do the thing we don't want them to do. Right. So somebody refunds and it's like, oh, reach out to them. Why did you refund? What could we have done better in all this thing? Right. And it's flawed thinking. Right. It's like what you want to optimize for the people who are doing the thing you want everybody to do. Right. When someone purchases something and they actually put it in the use and you see signals of that through comments and engagement and questions and participation and things like this, those are the people you want to find out. Optimize for them. Right. Don't optimize to try to get the people who are, you know, you're probably not going to get much effect if you don't want to get it. You know, you're probably not going to get much effect if you, especially with low ridiculously low refund rates, but even in an environment with, you know, somewhat high refund rates, don't I'm trying to remember the Dan Heath, Dan Chip Heath book about this. It's one of my favorite books, but they refer to optimizing for bright spots. Right. Figure out it's switch. Yeah. That's such a great book. They use the term bright spots. Like look for the bright spots in your business, the VIPs and optimize your business based on the bright spots. What do the people who do the thing, you know, when you're successful in your business, what like study that and then make more of your business, do that. Don't and it's such human nature to try to focus on the other end and to, you know, make like the things that are not going as well a little bit better. It's just it's a different way of thinking about it, but and it doesn't, it doesn't directly tie to this question. So I'll move on. All right. Karma asks, is it okay in the first email and an RBS to talk about your backstory or should it be dedicated to what your subscribers are going to get emails or both? I saw my first RBS email. I talked a lot about myself and then I read it again. I felt awkward and we all karma. And I talked about myself and it not sure if you would. I'm sorry. So. Hmm. You know, sort of my first thought about this is I, I try to be interested before I try to be interesting and I don't really try to be interesting. I just spend most of my time. Anything that if I'm trying to do something, it's I'm trying to be interested. That's sort of how I approach life. Because I am, I'm just naturally curious about everything. So I would that would be the framework through which I would think about answering this question. So I would in general, it's especially in that first introduction, the first connection, I would be trying to emphasize, I would be overemphasizing empathy and being interested in the other person and deemphasizing talking about myself. Now, it doesn't mean that's the right answer for you. Sometimes talking about it depends on how it's framed and what the expectations are. That really is the answer. However you framed the entry point into your world, the next thing that happens should be the next thing that makes most sense within that frame. So if you frame it as a, you know, I'm going to tell you about how I got here and why that's relevant to you when they get the first email and you tell them why you got there and how that's relevant to them, well, that's perfectly congruent. It's what they expected and you gave them that. But I think all things being equal in general, the more the first email is a sets the stage for the value that it's going to be about them. I think that's a better decision. So and just to be clear, we all feel awkward reading everything about ourselves, especially when we talk about ourselves, it's human nature. So that's not the way to, don't let that get in your way. The decision that you need, the framework you need to think about is if someone is new to your world, what is the most valuable thing that you can say to them first that frames the conversation that follows? And if that is saying to them in order to give you the most value over the next seven days, I need to take a step back and explain to you how I got here because that will be relevant to that conversation. That's perfectly OK. Just put that little process on there so that you're helping them orient the story into the larger narrative. I think that's how I would think about it. Andre, how would you think about that? Yeah, I'll give a very broad response. You're going to have to pick out the value from within it. So one way one one way that I've wrapped my head around this is by reading fantasy books. So there's a there's a guy you can go to YouTube, Brandon Sanderson, Sanderson, and he writes these epic fantasy books. They're amazing, like eight hundred pages for a single book, and it's part of a trilogy or as part of a series. And he's he's got some YouTube classes. He does it at a university where he unpacks different parts of the process. And he's got a really interesting one that's wrapped around world building and how we do how they do how infodumps are dead or are done badly and well. So you can think about if you want to give somebody your back story, that's kind of part of world building in the sense that maps to what Brandon is is saying in the fantasy world, where fantasy done badly in the beginning of the book, where it's trying to establish world building in a world that's different to what the readers normal world is. So there has to be moments where there's world building components have to be given to the reader so they can understand stuff. And when it's done badly, there's huge blocks of boring world building stuff. And then the story is is is kind of injected inside of that. And it's it's it's very jarring, it's boring, and it doesn't work well. And the way that masters do this, that are really good at their craft is they is they inject world building components in places where it where it makes sense and matters. But they'll do it quickly just enough so it contributes to to the narrative and actually makes the narrative stronger because now it's more clear that the person reading it understands that part of the world, that only to understand the entire world, to understand that part of the story. And I think you know, you can do the same when you're doing your back story stuff. It can get injected in not in a big block of text. And then you're trying to figure out how you're going to talk about them as opposed to talking about you. I think it can be all them, which is the story component and the world building component, which is the you part can get stuck inside there in a way that that makes that flows better. So just, you know, it's just it's difficult to explain without showing examples. And obviously, you know, reading a fantasy book, an 800 page fantasy book isn't isn't the easiest example to to give. But if that makes sense, that's kind of how how you can do that. So you can inject your bits of your of your back story inside of the context of an email that's mostly about them, but it's more powerful because in and amongst the mostly about them part is the stuff that's about you that makes mostly about them part more powerful. So that's my that's my answer. All right, move on to Guillermo's question. This is an agency specific question. I double checked that we didn't really answer this previously. But I think the thinking out loud about how to use the R&B now from an agency perspective earlier probably answers most of this. We didn't cover the entry point, the multi page presale site. The only thing I don't like this question at all is this is the me part, the me and the I part. You know, we put our, you know, yes, we, you know, as agency owners and as consultants, you know, we are at the center of things. But I don't think that's the way to think about this. Put the customer at this, put the ideal customer at the center. Right. So the ideal customer is trying to solve certain core number of issues or again, a certain set of gains. You can think about jobs, pains and gains if you want to. Your ideal customer, that that's where you want to meet them. Right. You don't meet them to talk. It sounds funny, but they're not there to talk about you. They're there to have you talk about them. Right. That's that's the distinction. So when they show up, talk about them and in talking about them. And I think there's and I can't remember the specifics on this, but I think there's some pretty compelling research that when two people communicate, if one person listens to the other talk about themselves for a long period of time, that the person who did all of the talking tends to feel like it was a really good conversation. I can't remember like the specifics, but it's kind of that. Right. Like if one person is just reflecting back on the other, what they're saying, the person who's doing all the talking feels like it was a really great conversation. That's very true in agency and consulting relationships. And so again, people are there. It sounds crazy. Like they need to know that your different, your approach is different. That on and on, but they that's not where you meet them. You meet them by establishing that you are a guide. Right. Luke Skywalker is showing up. Luke Skywalker didn't know anything about the Force. Didn't know anything about becoming a Jedi. I didn't know any of that stuff. But Luke Skywalker met Obi-Wan Kenobi, who met him where he was as a farmer and then showed him the world that could be available to him. At no point did Obi-Wan Kenobi talk about what a great Jedi he was. Right. Never. I mean, you didn't know until whatever he's on the Death Star and pulls out the lightsaber and has that it was Darth Vader. Like it was really only then that you started to think, oh, this guy was actually a Jedi too. Right. So and maybe that's not the best metaphor, but I kind of like that metaphor. Right. Like when you show when that your ideal customers show up, meet them exactly where they are and then begin to show them what could be and what's appropriate at that point and continue to do that. But not. You're not focusing on you. It's always about them and how you can help them or how you've helped others like them. And then when you get to the value newsletter, then at that point, you sort of established yourself as a guy to the tips and the ideas and others are coming from this perspective. It's the same thing my dad taught me when I was younger. I think I've shared this elsewhere. My dad described it this way. We were at I was in fifth grade. We were at a martial arts event. My father was a when he died, he was a legitimate eighth three black belt of one of two non Korean black belts at that rank and take one over the world at the time we died. Not a Mac Dojo printed out certificate, the real deal. When I was in fifth grade, I was not a martial arts at the time. My father was we went to a tournament and the person who was running the tournament had just achieved the rank where his title changed from Mr. to master. It's very common when this happens that people for 25 plus years have referred to you as Mr. Now, then they see you and they just forget and they call you Mr. No, no disrespect is intended. But this person, several people had called him Mr. And he jumped up on a table and I remember this very distinctly. It was wagging his finger at this group of black belts and me for hanging on to my dad's arm. But this wagging his finger saying, you know, I am a master and you will address me as such. And my dad looked at me and he said, you're going to meet two kinds of people in your life. You will meet people like that who demand your respect. And then you will meet other people who command your respect. Don't demand people's respect. So when we talk about ourselves, when someone shows up and we say, hey, not only am I an expert, I am perfectly equipped to tell you all of the ways in which I am an expert while demonstrating my expertise. We're demanding respect. Don't tell people you're an expert, be an expert. And what experts do is they meet the people who they're who are coming to them for expertise where they are and then they take them on a journey. At no point does a true expert ever have to tell somebody else that they're an expert, the expertise is evident. So I would I would suggest that that's the short answer to this question, which is meet your prospects where they are and take them on a journey where they are the evidence of your expertise reveals itself so clearly yet in no way could somebody point to it and say, this is where he told me how smart he is. That's how I would think about this question. André, any thoughts to add for this one? No, no, really. I have only got two words, which is demonstrate expertise. And that's that's the short version of what you've just said. Yeah, there you go. Cool. I love this next question. We certainly have personality that we use in our market, but we don't we don't make it about us. I don't think and if we do, it's an accident. But can it be useful? Absolutely, it can be useful. So cult of personality. If you want to cultivate that and you're willing to pay the consequences, which is what you have to put yourself in the public spotlight. And, you know, that's the work of Amos Tversky and Danny Coniman of behavioral economics. Tons of psychological insights that we can use that are just that's human nature's reality, it's gravity. So, yeah, we should learn about this stuff. But learning about psychology of customers so that we can become a favorite talking head, but then not being able to lie. That's a lot of constraints. Oh, I got it. I mean, if I want to be their favorite talking head and get it in, I want to be able to lie like politicians. So I feel a little constrained here, but I just this is a fun question. In many ways, I feel like, you know, maybe one of us could have written it and dropped it in here just to be a funny thing to answer. But the broader thing is here, when you do this, whatever choices that you make, this is a broad this is a broad answer to a lot of questions. Whatever choices you make for your business, you have to live with. And when you make those choices, open your eyes wide and ask yourself if this is wildly successful, is this the business that I want to live with? Right. So karma's question earlier. There's a PLS better than a story powered promotion. There's generally probably no direct answer to that. But PLS, a business based around product launch series is a different business than different demands in the business that's automated based around story powered promotions. Each of those decisions has consequences. Are you willing to live with those consequences? Lying your ass off to sell stuff online and getting people pissed off and trying to make everybody else bad while you're good and trying to figure out the latest psychological hack to get people to like you so they buy your shit. I want I want nothing to do with that. I want I don't want to learn anything from that. All I know about that is that I don't want it and that's enough. Right. Like so, that's the short answer. I guess I could have gone to just took me a while to get there. What a fun question. Holy shit. You know, one of the things that we embrace is J. Abraham's strategy of preeminence. The part we don't talk about in the strategy of preeminence is that we have an ethical responsibility to encourage our customers to buy at a frequency and rate where they get the most frequency and sort of breadth where they get the most value. Right. That's part of strategy of preeminence. And I've had this conversation a lot lately. A couple of weeks ago, I think it was John Carlin who really nailed this. I can have the quote somewhere that I can share later. But what John said, and it was sort of the theme and others have said it, and it's really important if you have an ethical business that sells a quality product, so first of all, just that the thing that you creates adds value to the world, you have an ethical responsibility to encourage people who will benefit from that value to pay for it, essentially. There's a spectrum here. It doesn't mean you get people who shouldn't buy for whatever reason buy, but you do in that scenario have a clear if what you have created will make your prospects life better, objectively better, you do have a responsibility to sell that thing to them. So I could I could imagine creating a relationship building series where, you know, I sort of frame it so, you know, and I don't know Julie's experience in details, I'll just make things up and say, you know, what, what just in Julie has 25 years experience working with people to understand natural movement. So I might say something like, you know, in the 25 years that I've been working with clients to better understand how to move naturally, I've noticed that there are three common mistakes that trip everybody up and also three things, three decisions that they can make that can dramatically improve how they whatever XYZ. Well, in that, like right there now, I mean, now my attention is like, I want to know what are those six things, right? Like I can't like that. My mind is focused on those. And as each of the in just at an initial level, that has begun to pull my attention forward because it's set up something that's all about what you're going to tell me within the frame of this credibility and experience. And then as you begin to tell me those things, and you might even then amplify and say, today we're going to focus on the first of three common mistakes, first of the three most common mistakes. Now, like now I'm writing copy, I want to go find out the answer to that's made up. So that's how I kind of know I'm on the right track. Right. Like so now, now, now my attention is like, like you're driving me crazy because it's like you're going to tell me all of these things. But now only today you're going to tell me one of these things. But the thing you're telling me today is important, but it's not even as important as the thing you're going to tell me next. Shit. Like, like I don't know what to do. Like now I'm just like I'm in it. Right. And it's just doing that all the time. It's just kind of like you're you're you're educating and communicating and directing attention to what's coming next and orienting and you're doing all of it. I mean, if we just said what's going to come next, you know, we would be like every, you know, gnarly marketer in the world. This is why if you're if everyone listening is called a Trump supporter, but don't take offense at this, this is an observation. Trump's a marketer. Right. That's his background. He's a marketer. But one thing Donald Trump did as president that irritated the hell out of me because I've seen so many marketers do this is he was always telling me about what was going to happen, but nothing ever happened. Right. Infrastructure week never happened. Right. But it was always going to be the best infrastructure week. We were going to have everything was always going to be the best. But nothing ever happened. I know so many marketers who are like that, that they're what they're going to teach you is going to absolutely change things. When you buy into their mastermind, your business is going to 10 X and then next time next meeting, we're going to cover these things. That's going to be the thing. But nothing ever actually does it. And that drives people that shit crazy. So we have to balance the directing the. I think a lot of marketers are really good at directing attention to the future and never actually getting there and thinking that's the point. That's not the point. The point is to balance orienting people to where they are, orienting to them. How far you've taken them so far, showing them what's coming next and then giving them some value now, recovering every single time frame all the time in our communication and we can't leave out any of them. If we talk too much about the right now, then we're not pulling attention forward. If we talk too much about what's going to happen, we never actually make anything happen. So it's finding that balance in all of that. That's that's the big challenge. It's not it's not just the pulling the attention forward. It's the pulling the attention forward while then bringing them back to the present moment to give them some value to then pull their attention forward again. And then when you have their attention the next day to remind them of the value that they got to reorient them, it's all of that. I hope I hope that makes sense. Yeah, I don't know. That's how I would think about that one. Yeah, I like that. It's good. I want to move on to the next one. So I think you put them all in here. I got you. Yeah, I would forward it. You know, this showed up in the earlier versions of of Oris wonder madness, where I used to talk about the PS being an area where you can have these little subplots that are all personal stories about the dog and stuff and people. I mean, it's weird when you when you do these suddenly responses come into in via email, they've, you know, none of the responses about the the amazing value inside the main part of the email and everyone's making comments on the little personal piece that's that's in the PS. So people definitely do connect with that. I mean, it can obviously be overdone. So, you know, one of the things that we like to do is sometimes in the PS will be, you know, PS, this is from Andre, PPS, this is from Sean. And we'll just inject something that adds a bit more texture to to the main email or can be completely different. It's just it's just that piece of personality. So, yeah, I mean, there's no right or wrong way to do that. That's that's just how we've done it. Yeah, I do remember one example where this was done wrong, where I won't I won't mention the person's name. I think he's still around. I took the core like I spent some money. I bought the book, spent some money on the course, like real money. Went through it was good a long time ago. And the person who was the main person months later, six months plus later, apparently was getting divorced, got divorced. But I remember I got a a faux personal postcard in the mail. I mean, faux personal, meaning I had the thing that was supposed to look like handwriting, but I wasn't handwriting. It was like whatever some service, but it was a postcard from the person telling like thanking me for being there through his divorce. And I was like, first of all, didn't know you were getting divorced. Second of all, that's not the nature of the relationship that we have. Third, this attempt at a personal communication that isn't personal. Like the whole thing, everything about my opinion of that person and that one moment changed for the worst. So and to this day, this is like 15 years ago, to me, that still stands out as like a how not to do things. And also, I want to be respectful of the fact that that's one of the most stressful situations that human being can go through his divorce. So I want to give the person a little I should be a little kinder, give them a little more latitude. But, you know, if that's then just be aware of the nature of the relationship that you have with your audience and kind of, yeah, there's there's lots of there's a spectrum in there, but just in general, don't send people fake postcards about your divorce if that's not the nature of your relationship. I was leaving it at that, I guess it was such a weird experience. All right. Looks like we have two more questions. Karma's isn't here twice. Maybe we had two more questions. Yeah. All right. Maybe you need to be there saying, I'm you know, I know what you need and here's what it is. And this is how I know. Maybe that works. I mean, that that's the Grant Cardone model. But in general, I think it's I think empathy trumps credibility in general or empathy in early get empathy, right? Credibility works, get empathy, wrong. Credibility is a crapshoot. You just don't know if it's going to work. So to me, empathy is the great and a leverage point when you is when you can describe someone to someone, their situation and what they're feeling in a way that it becomes actionable and solvable and there's hope. That's credible. But credibility emerges from the empathy, whereas empathy generally does not emerge from credibility. It can it can feel a little colder. That's just that's the only nuance I would add to this. Cool. You don't have anything to add. No, damn. Karma, we answer this last question. Oh, my God, what happened here? And he tells the story of how he created a newsletter where I think he charged four hundred and fifty dollars a year for it. He didn't know the answers to, but he was committed to finding the answers to. And he already had an email list so that that's a factor as well. But he I don't know how much money he generated. Right. I mean, you could also sell. I think I mean, we're seeing a renaissance of newsletters. But I think there's something really magical about saying, here's what I'm interested in, like the assuming the questions are interesting. If your audience looks at him and is like, I don't know, interested in that thing, they're not going to buy it. But if someone in my field put together a list of 55 questions and said, you know what, these are important, I'm going to spend a significant amount of time answering each of these to my own satisfaction and then publishing the results and sharing what I find, I would sign up for that fast, like fast, fast, fast. So I think this can be done. But the only challenge here, there's a little price compression in the newsletter market, there are a million newsletters. So I don't know that I would call it a newsletter. I would probably call it something different. But I would, there's just something magical about that particular format. I mean, yeah, I don't really have to unpack the whole newsletter thing. They're everywhere, paid ones, free ones. You can look at the price points. They all fairly low, normally a yearly fee. So, you know, just, you know, if you want to become part of the herd, then that's a great strategy. But Sean hinted at something and that it's called it's, you know, how do you create a category of one? Well, call it something else. So, so the mechanism can be the same. It's it's it's information being dispensed over email or PDF through email or whatever. But it's or something gated up. It's just that, you know, you can call it a newsletter, but then you're going to be constrained by how people are framing what this is. So, you know, there's there's only so much you can you can charge for for a mass market newsletter, even if it is a somewhat nichey thing. But yeah, if you call it something else, you can charge you can charge a complete different price because now it's it's in its own category. So one example is my friend Peter Sparpen who passed away. Him and I did the tiny as my tea thing. And, you know, that was essentially a newsletter done. Broadly one thing per per month over a year, it was over 10 months. Peter didn't quite make it all the way through to the 10 months. But, you know, we charged 40 or 50 dollars a month for that. So five hundred for the for the 10. So, you know, that that was a newsletter that wasn't called a newsletter. Yeah, so just I think that's that's the takeaway is maybe call it something else. If you want to price it differently or, you know, for people not to price shop or compare it to something else. Yeah, but that's it. That's what I've got. Yeah, I think the only nuance I would add to that, too, sort of something I read in a book years ago that's so sunk in with me. That's really just. I keep coming back to it. The book is called Diamines, D I A M I N D S. It's one of those books I read a few times. It's one of those books that it's an end of the for me, at least it's an intellectual struggle right up against my abilities all the time with it. And it's but it's amazing. But there's in the it's somewhere in the book, the authors make this point, which is a lot of business success comes down to positioning something that's very valuable with an audience that is willing to pay a lot for it. Right. Sounds obvious, but it's so that the nuance is so rich. So from a newsletter perspective, I mean, you can you can create something that's mildly interesting or kind of interesting or mildly entertaining or very entertaining for a large group of people and make a business from that. That's one business model. That's probably a ten dollar a month subscription based newsletter. And that makes perfect sense. As long as it is that there's lots of people interested in that. But then you can also at the other end of the spectrum, you can have this list of questions or however you think about its topics. You can have something that is really important to a small group of people who are willing to pay a lot of money for it. That's and then everything else exists in between. Right. So one of the questions to ask yourself and I believe it begins with you first is do you want to be in the little bit for lots of people business or a little bit for a few people or depth and breadth for a few people? That's a starting point to think about, especially in this particular format. And then when you get to and also what can you deliver on? Right. Like if you're going to if you want to go into a so if you want to go into a marketing niche, do you have the experience and the expertise to really deliver on that hyper hyper focus, like really hard question, the intractable questions that few people at the absolute upper end want to focus on. But those few people will pay any price to get the answers to the questions. And once the answers are really well thought out, like those are very different businesses and they they should reflect what you're most interested in and what you're most capable of. It's not enough to say, I just want to work with the people and we see this all the time in the service provider. I only want to work with the top top one percent. Well, can you solve the problems that the one the top one percent have? And if you can't, then you're not going to work with the top one percent. Right. Like their problems are different in general. You know, maybe similar problems, but they require different depths of expertise to answer them. So that I would just add that nuance there too, just for everybody. All right.
